issues.description: Q3 pricing tier example conflicts with Q4 reference account sizing. Q3 states '3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/mo)' but also lists '5,000 endpoints: $11/seat/month'. Per Q3's own rule (mid-tier counts price at the next-lower-tier rate), Apex Insurance at 5,100 endpoints would price at the 5,000-tier ($11), and Meridian National at 3,200 would price at $15 — this is internally consistent, but a prospect doing the math on Crestview Capital (850 endpoints, Q4) would correctly infer they price at the $18 rate, which may surprise buyers expecting volume pricing at sub-1,000 scale. Worth clarifying in pricing narrative.

Q2 mentions 'EU data residency' in the context of the NCC Group penetration test, which is an odd pairing — pen testing is not a data residency control. Q5 correctly frames NCC Group testing as general security assurance. Not a factual contradiction, but the Q2 phrasing could confuse a compliance reviewer.

Q3 pricing rules state mid-tier endpoint counts price at the next-lower tier, with the next discount tier reached only at 1,000 or 5,000 thresholds. This implies 3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier ($15/seat/mo) and 5,100 endpoints would price at the 5,000-tier ($11/seat/mo). Q4 references customers with 3,200 endpoints (Meridian National) and 5,100 endpoints (Apex Insurance) — these are example reference accounts, not pricing claims, so this is not a direct contradiction, but a prospect cross-referencing may ask. Note: Crestview Capital at 850 endpoints aligns with the 500-tier example given in Q3.

Q4 lists Crestview Capital at 850 endpoints (asset management) and Meridian National at 3,200 endpoints (bank). Q3 uses these exact same numbers (850 and 3,200) as illustrative pricing tier examples. This is not a factual contradiction, but a prospect cross-reading both answers may notice the coincidence and question whether the reference accounts are real or fabricated examples.

Q3 states mid-tier endpoint counts are priced at the next-lower tier (e.g., 3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier rate of $15/seat/month). Q4 lists Apex Insurance at 5,100 endpoints and Meridian National at 3,200 endpoints — internally consistent with Q3's tier rules, but a prospect comparing the reference customer Crestview Capital (850 endpoints) to the pricing example (850 endpoints → $18/seat) will tie the two answers together cleanly. No factual contradiction, but worth noting alignment is tight and any future change to one answer must be mirrored.

Q4 lists Crestview Capital at 850 endpoints and Apex Insurance at 5,100 endpoints as reference accounts, but Q3's pricing tiers state that 850 endpoints would price at the 500-tier rate ($18/seat/mo) and 5,100 endpoints would just cross the 5,000-tier threshold ($11/seat/mo). This is not a direct contradiction but a prospect may ask why reference customers sit just below/above tier breakpoints — worth verifying actual contracted rates align with the published tier logic.

Q3 states that to qualify for a tier's rate, the endpoint count must meet or exceed that tier's threshold, and uses 3,200 endpoints as an example billed at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/mo). Q4 lists Apex Insurance at 5,100 endpoints and Meridian National at 3,200 endpoints — these are reference accounts, not pricing claims, so no direct contradiction, but a prospect comparing the 5,000-tier example pricing may ask whether Apex (5,100) qualifies for the 5,000-tier. This is a clarity issue, not a factual contradiction.

Q4 lists Apex Insurance with ~5,100 endpoints, which falls between the 1,000 and 5,000 pricing tiers defined in Q3. Per Q3's tier rules, 5,100 endpoints would price at the 5,000-tier rate ($11/seat/month). This is not a direct contradiction but a prospect might ask whether Apex receives the 5,000-tier discount given it just exceeds the threshold. No action required unless pricing for that reference is disclosed.

Q1 refers to the included engine as a 'correlation engine rated at 50,000 EPS' (part of base EPP+EDR), while Q3 calls it 'our built-in correlation engine' included in the EPP+EDR bundle. Both are consistent in scope (built-in vs. paid SIEM add-on); no contradiction.

Q4 lists Crestview Capital as an asset management firm with 850 endpoints (customer since 2023) and Meridian National as a bank with 3,200 endpoints. In Q3, the same endpoint counts (850 and 3,200) are used as pricing examples to illustrate tier rounding. A prospect comparing the two answers may notice the suspicious overlap and question whether the references are real or hypothetical.

Q4 states Crestview Capital has 850 endpoints and Apex Insurance has 5,100 endpoints, but Q3's pricing example uses '850 endpoints would price at the 500-tier' and '3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier'. While not directly contradictory, the reference customer endpoint counts (850, 3,200, 5,100) appearing as exact pricing-tier illustration values is suspicious and a savvy prospect may notice. Verify these are real customer counts, not fabricated examples.

Q1 attributes the 90-day telemetry / 13-month alert retention to 'Helios Data Sovereignty v3.1', which is consistent with Q5. No contradiction, but Q2 also cites the same document for the NCC Group pen test, while Q5 states the pen test is annual — all consistent. No action needed.

Q3 states that mid-tier endpoint counts price at the next-lower tier's rate, with 3,200 endpoints billed at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/mo). Q4 lists Apex Insurance as a customer with 5,100 endpoints — just above the 5,000-tier threshold — which is consistent. However, Crestview Capital (850 endpoints) and Meridian National (3,200 endpoints) are referenced without pricing implications, so no direct contradiction exists. Worth verifying that reference customer endpoint counts align with pricing tier examples used.

Q4 lists Crestview Capital (Asset Management) at ~850 endpoints and Meridian National (Banking) at ~3,200 endpoints. Q3's pricing example states that 850 endpoints would be billed at the 500-tier rate ($18/seat/mo) and 3,200 endpoints at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/mo). While not strictly a contradiction, a prospect comparing these numbers may notice the reference customer endpoint counts exactly match the pricing examples, which could appear fabricated. Worth verifying the reference account figures are accurate.

Q3 states that mid-tier endpoint counts are priced at the next-lower tier and that a deployment must reach exactly 1,000 endpoints to qualify for the 1,000-EP rate (so 3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier rate of $15/seat/mo, not the 5,000-tier). However, Q4 lists Meridian National with 3,200 endpoints and Crestview Capital with 850 endpoints — these counts fall between published tiers. While not directly contradictory on price, prospects doing the math will note that reference customers sit at awkward mid-tier sizes that Q3's pricing rules explicitly call out as not qualifying for better rates. More critically, Apex Insurance is listed at 5,100 endpoints — just above the 5,000 threshold — which a prospect may scrutinize.

Q3's stated rule that a customer must reach 'exactly 1,000 endpoints' (not 1,000+) to qualify for the 1,000-tier rate is unusual and internally awkward — it implies 1,001 endpoints would not qualify. This is likely a wording issue rather than a cross-answer contradiction, but a prospect would likely raise it.

Q3 states mid-tier endpoint counts price at the next-lower tier's rate, giving the example that a 3,200-endpoint deployment prices at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/mo). Q4 lists Meridian National as a banking customer with 3,200 endpoints and Crestview Capital with 850 endpoints — these specific customer sizes will map to the higher (less-discounted) tiers per Q3's rule. Not a contradiction between answers, but a prospect cross-referencing Q3's example against Q4's named accounts may notice the example uses a real customer's endpoint count, which could be awkward.

Multi-year terms adding 5%/10% rather than discounting is flagged within Q3 itself but not contradicted elsewhere. Worth human confirmation before submission as already noted in the answer's own flags.

Q3 states that 3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/mo) because the count must meet or exceed the 5,000 threshold to qualify for that tier. Q4 lists Apex Insurance as a customer with 5,100 endpoints and Meridian National with 3,200 endpoints — these are reference customers, not pricing claims, so this is consistent, but a prospect may ask whether Apex (5,100) qualifies for 5,000-tier pricing. No direct contradiction, just worth noting.

Q3 states that mid-tier endpoint counts are billed at the next-lower-tier rate, giving the explicit example that 'a 3,200-endpoint environment would be billed at the 1,000-endpoint rate ($15/seat/month).' However, Q4 lists Meridian National as a reference account with 3,200 endpoints — a prospect comparing their own 3,200-endpoint scenario to this reference may ask why pricing tiers jump directly from 1,000 to 5,000 with no 3,000-endpoint tier, and whether reference customers received different pricing. Not a direct factual contradiction but a likely prospect-facing inconsistency to reconcile.

Q3 states 3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier rate of $15/seat/month, but Q4 lists Meridian National as a customer with 3,200 endpoints and Apex Insurance with 5,100 endpoints. While not a direct contradiction, a prospect comparing pricing tiers to reference customer sizes may ask about actual contract pricing — worth verifying alignment but not a factual contradiction in the answers themselves.

Q3 states mid-tier endpoint counts are billed at the next-lower tier (e.g., 3,200 endpoints billed at the 1,000-tier rate of $15/seat/mo). Q4 lists Apex Insurance at 5,100 endpoints and Meridian National at 3,200 endpoints — not a direct contradiction, but a prospect cross-checking pricing against the named reference accounts may ask why a 5,100-endpoint customer isn't quoted at the 5,000 tier. Worth confirming pricing tier application is consistent with how reference accounts are billed.

Q3 states that a deployment of 3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/mo) because volume discounts only apply once the next tier threshold is reached. However, the 5,000-endpoint tier ($11/seat/mo) implies the threshold is 5,000. Reference customer Meridian National (Q4) has 3,200 endpoints, and Apex Insurance has 5,100 endpoints — a prospect comparing these may ask why 3,200 doesn't qualify for the 5,000 tier. This is internally consistent but the pricing example using 3,200 endpoints (matching a named reference customer) could invite scrutiny.

Q3 pricing tier mapping conflicts with Q4 reference account sizes. Q3 states mid-tier counts price at the next-lower tier (e.g., 850 endpoints = $18/seat, 3,200 endpoints = $15/seat). Q4 lists Crestview Capital at ~850 endpoints and Meridian National at ~3,200 endpoints — these are illustrative numbers that exactly match Q3's pricing examples, which a prospect may notice as suspiciously coincidental rather than real reference data.

Q3 pricing tiers state that a 3,200-endpoint deployment is priced at the 1,000-tier rate ($15/seat/month), implying customers between 1,000 and 5,000 endpoints fall into the 1,000 tier. However, Q4 lists Apex Insurance with 5,100 endpoints and Meridian National with 3,200 endpoints as reference accounts — the 3,200 figure exactly matches the example in Q3, which a prospect may notice as a coincidence but is not a direct contradiction. No factual conflict, but worth noting for consistency.

Q3 states an 850-endpoint deployment would be priced at the 500-tier rate ($18/seat/mo), but Q4 lists Crestview Capital as a customer with exactly 850 endpoints. While not a direct factual contradiction, a prospect doing the math may ask whether Crestview is paying the 500-tier rate, and the example chosen in Q3 inadvertently mirrors a named reference account's seat count. Consider changing the Q3 illustrative example to avoid implying reference customer pricing.

Q4 lists Apex Insurance at 5,100 endpoints, which is just above the 5,000-tier threshold in Q3. Not a contradiction, but a prospect may probe whether Apex receives the 5,000-tier rate ($11/seat/mo). Consistent with the stated tier rules, so no change required unless additional context is desired.

Q3 states that to qualify for the 5,000-endpoint rate, the deployment must reach the 5,000-seat threshold, and uses 3,200 endpoints as an example that would be priced at the 1,000-tier rate. Q4 lists Apex Insurance as a reference at 5,100 endpoints and Meridian National at 3,200 endpoints — these are customer counts, not pricing claims, so this is consistent. However, prospects may cross-reference the 3,200-endpoint example in Q3 with Meridian National's 3,200 endpoints in Q4; this is illustrative overlap, not a contradiction.

Q3 pricing rules state that mid-tier endpoint counts price at the next-lower tier (e.g., 3,200 endpoints price at the 1,000-tier of $15/seat/month, and 850 endpoints price at the 500-tier of $18/seat/month). Q4 lists reference customers Meridian National (3,200 endpoints) and Crestview Capital (850 endpoints). While not a direct factual contradiction, a prospect comparing these answers may probe whether the published tier structure aligns with what these reference customers actually pay. No issue if pricing examples in Q3 were chosen deliberately; worth confirming.

Q3 pricing tiers conflict with Q4 reference customer sizes. Q3 states the 5,000-endpoint tier ($11/seat/mo) is reached 'only upon hitting the threshold' of 5,000 endpoints, implying Apex Insurance (5,100 endpoints) qualifies. However, Q3 also gives an example that '3,200-endpoint deployment would price at the 1,000-tier rate' — consistent. The issue: Q4 lists Meridian National at 3,200 endpoints and Crestview Capital at 850 endpoints, which aligns with Q3's examples (possibly too neatly — the examples in Q3 mirror actual customer sizes in Q4, which is internally consistent but worth verifying these aren't fabricated illustrative examples).

Q4 lists Crestview Capital as a reference customer with 850 endpoints, and Q3 uses '850 endpoints' as the example for mid-tier pricing landing at the 500-tier rate ($18/seat/mo). This is internally consistent on pricing, but Q4 also lists Apex Insurance at 5,100 endpoints — which under the Q3 tiering rules would price at the 5,000-tier ($11/seat/mo). No contradiction, but a prospect may probe whether reference accounts illustrate actual pricing applied. Worth confirming.

Q1 states the built-in correlation engine handles 'up to 50,000 EPS per tenant' as part of base EPP+EDR, while Q3 describes the built-in correlation engine as included but does not mention scale limits. Not a contradiction, but a prospect comparing technical and pricing answers may ask whether the 50K EPS cap applies uniformly across all pricing tiers (including 5,000-endpoint deployments).

Q4 lists Crestview Capital with 850 endpoints and Meridian National with 3,200 endpoints as reference customers. Q3's pricing example uses these exact same numbers (850 and 3,200) to illustrate mid-tier pricing behavior. While not a factual contradiction, a prospect may notice the coincidence and question whether the reference customer figures are real or illustrative. Worth verifying.

Q3 states that 3,200 endpoints would price at the 1,000-tier ($15/seat/mo) because volume discounts apply only when fully reaching the next tier. Q4 lists Meridian National as a banking reference with 3,200 endpoints. While not a direct contradiction, a prospect comparing these answers may ask why a 3,200-endpoint customer doesn't qualify for the 5,000-tier rate—this is consistent but worth noting for sales alignment.
